lines.greek: οἱ δʼ εἰς ὀρχηστύν τε καὶ ἱμερόεσσαν ἀοιδὴν
τρεψάμενοι τέρποντο, μένον δʼ ἐπὶ ἕσπερον ἐλθεῖν.
τοῖσι δὲ τερπομένοισι μέλας ἐπὶ ἕσπερος ἦλθε·
δὴ τότε κακκείοντες ἔβαν οἶκόνδε ἕκαστος.
Τηλέμαχος δʼ, ὅθι οἱ θάλαμος περικαλλέος αὐλῆς
ὑψηλὸς δέδμητο περισκέπτῳ ἐνὶ χώρῳ,
ἔνθʼ ἔβη εἰς εὐνὴν πολλὰ φρεσὶ μερμηρίζων.
τῷ δʼ ἄρʼ ἅμʼ αἰθομένας δαΐδας φέρε κεδνὰ ἰδυῖα
Εὐρύκλειʼ, Ὦπος θυγάτηρ Πεισηνορίδαο,
τήν ποτε Λαέρτης πρίατο κτεάτεσσιν ἑοῖσιν
πρωθήβην ἔτʼ ἐοῦσαν, ἐεικοσάβοια δʼ ἔδωκεν,
ἶσα δέ μιν κεδνῇ ἀλόχῳ τίεν ἐν μεγάροισιν,
εὐνῇ δʼ οὔ ποτʼ ἔμικτο, χόλον δʼ ἀλέεινε γυναικός·
ἥ οἱ ἅμʼ αἰθομένας δαΐδας φέρε, καί ἑ μάλιστα
δμῳάων φιλέεσκε, καὶ ἔτρεφε τυτθὸν ἐόντα.
ὤιξεν δὲ θύρας θαλάμου πύκα ποιητοῖο,
ἕζετο δʼ ἐν λέκτρῳ, μαλακὸν δʼ ἔκδυνε χιτῶνα·
καὶ τὸν μὲν γραίης πυκιμηδέος ἔμβαλε χερσίν.
ἡ μὲν τὸν πτύξασα καὶ ἀσκήσασα χιτῶνα,
πασσάλῳ ἀγκρεμάσασα παρὰ τρητοῖσι λέχεσσι

interlinear: and-they to dance and to-lovely song
having-turned, took-delight, and-waited for-evening to-come.
and-to-them, delighting-themselves, black evening came;
then indeed, to-lie-down, they-went, to-his-house each.
and-Telemachus, where for-him a-chamber of-the-beautiful court,
lofty, had-been-built, in a-far-seen place,
there he-went to-bed, many-things in his-mind pondering.
and-with-him blazing torches bore, kindly-minded
Eurycleia, of-Ops daughter, son-of-Peisenor,
whom once Laertes bought with-his-possessions,
in-first-youth still being; and-twenty-oxen'-worth he-gave,
and-equally her with-his-true wife he-honoured in-the-halls,
but-in-bed never he-lay-with-her, and-the-anger he-avoided of-his-wife;
she for-him blazing torches bore, and him most-of-all
of-the-slave-women she-loved, and reared-him, little being.
and-she-opened the-doors of-the-chamber, strongly built,
and-he-sat on the-bed, and-off he-took his-soft tunic,
and-it into the-old-woman's, shrewd-minded, hands he-put.
and-she, having-folded and having-smoothed the-tunic,
on-a-peg having-hung-it beside the-corded bed,
literal: Then they turned to the dance and to lovely song
and took their delight, and waited for evening to come;
and as they delighted, black evening came upon them.
Then they went, each to his own house, to lie down.
But Telemachus, where his chamber had been built,
lofty, in the beautiful court, in a place of wide outlook,
there he went to his bed, pondering many things in his mind.
And with him, bearing blazing torches, went kindly-minded
Eurycleia, daughter of Ops, son of Peisenor,
whom Laertes had once bought with his own possessions,
when she was still in her first youth, and gave twenty oxen for her;
and he honoured her in the halls equally with his true wife,
but never lay with her, and avoided his wife's anger.
She bore the blazing torches for him, and loved him most
of all the slave-women, and had reared him when he was little.
She opened the doors of the strongly built chamber,
and he sat on the bed and took off his soft tunic,
and put it into the hands of the shrewd old woman.
And she folded and smoothed the tunic,
and hung it on a peg beside the corded bed,
translations.elizabethan:
Then to the dance they turned, and lovely song,
and made them merry, waiting for the dusk;
and while they took their pleasure, evening came,
black evening; and they went then, every man
unto his house, to lay him down and sleep.
Telemachus, where in the beautiful court
his chamber had been builded, high, and set
in a wide-looking place, went there to bed,
and turned much over in his mind. With him
went kindly-hearted Eurycleia, bearing
the blazing torches, Ops' daughter she,
the son of Peisenor; whom long ago
Laertes bought out of his own possessions,
when she was in her earliest youth, and gave
the worth of twenty oxen; and he held her
in honour in the halls as his true wife,
but never lay with her, and shunned the wrath
of his own lady. She it was that bore
the torches for him, and of all the women
loved him the best, and nursed him when a child.
She opened then the strong-built chamber doors;
he sat upon the bed and drew away
his soft tunic, and laid it in her hands,
the shrewd old woman's; and she folded it
and smoothed it, hung it on a peg beside
the corded bedstead.
translations.modernist:
Then they turned to dancing and to the songs they liked, and enjoyed themselves, and waited for the evening. While they were enjoying themselves the black evening came. Then they went home, each man to his own house, to lie down.

Telemachus went to bed in his own room. It was built high in the beautiful court, in a place you could see a long way from. He lay there and turned a great many things over in his mind.

Eurycleia went with him carrying the burning torches. She was the daughter of Ops, the son of Peisenor. Laertes bought her a long time ago with his own money, when she was very young. He paid twenty oxen for her. He gave her the same honor in the house as his own wife, but he never slept with her. He kept clear of his wife's anger.

She carried the torches for him. She loved him more than any of the other women did, and she had brought him up when he was small.

She opened the doors of the strong room. He sat down on the bed and pulled off his soft tunic and put it in the old woman's hands. She was shrewd. She folded the tunic and smoothed it out and hung it on a peg by the corded bed.
translations.storybook:
Then they all turned to dancing and lovely singing, and enjoyed themselves, and waited for the evening to come. And while they were enjoying themselves, the black evening did come. So off they went, each man to his own house, to lie down and sleep.

Telemachus went to his own bedroom, which was built up high in the beautiful courtyard, in a spot where you could see for miles. He lay down there with a great many things turning over in his mind.

Kind old Eurycleia went with him, carrying the blazing torches. She was the daughter of Ops, the son of Peisenor. Long ago, Laertes had bought her with his own money when she was still very young, and paid twenty oxen for her, and he always treated her as respectfully as his own wife, though he never took her to his bed, because he did not want to make his wife angry.

She was the one who carried the torches for Telemachus, and she loved him best of all the servants in the house, for she had looked after him when he was a tiny boy.

She opened the doors of the sturdy room, and he sat down on the bed and pulled off his soft tunic and put it into the clever old woman's hands. She folded it up and smoothed it flat, and hung it on a peg beside the corded bed.

murray.text: But Telemachus, where his chamber was built in the beautiful court, high, in a place of wide outlook, thither went to his bed, pondering many things in mind; and with him, bearing blazing torches, went true-hearted Eurycleia, daughter of Ops, son of Peisenor.

Her long ago Laertes had bought with his wealth, when she was in her first youth, and gave for her the price of twenty oxen; and he honored her even as he honored his faithful wife in his halls, but he never lay with her in love, for he shunned the wrath of his wife. She it was who bore for Telemachus the blazing torches;

for she of all the handmaids loved him most, and had nursed him when he was a child. He opened the doors of the well-built chamber, sat down on the bed, and took off his soft tunic and laid it in the wise old woman's hands. And she folded and smoothed the tunic

and hung it on a peg beside the corded bedstead, and then went forth from the chamber, drawing the door to by its silver handle, and driving the bolt home with the thong. So there, the night through, wrapped in a fleece of wool, he pondered in his mind upon the journey which Athena had shewn him.
